name: Saint-Girons - Foix Greenway
name_original: Sur la voie verte du Piémont Pyrénéen
description: Cycle the moderate Saint-Girons - Foix Greenway (V81), a 18.3-mile route through the Pyrénées ariégeoises with tunnels and Pyrenees views.
region_intro: No traffic touring cycling routes in Pyrénées Ariégeoises traverse a region characterized by its diverse landscapes, from challenging mountain passes to gentle greenways along river valleys. This area, part of the Parc Naturel Régional des Pyrénées Ariégeoises, offers panoramic views of mountains, deep valleys, and lush forests. Cyclists can explore quiet, often traffic-free roads through varied terrain, including former railway lines converted into "Voie Verte" paths. The region's natural features provide a range of experiences for touring cyclists of all levels. Best no traffic touring cycling routes in Pyrénées Ariégeoises The most popular no traffic touring cycling route is Saint-Girons - Foix Greenway, an 18.3 miles (29.5 km) trail that takes 1 hour 55 minutes to complete. This moderate route follows a former railway line, offering gentle slopes and picturesque views. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is Discovering Notre-Dame de la Goutte in Montardit, a difficult 16.3 miles (26.2 km) path. This route features significant elevation gain, winding through the region's hilly terrain. Local touring cyclists also love the Catchaudégué Pass – Col de Catchaudégué (893 m) loop from Alos, a 23.2 miles (37.3 km) trail leading through mountain passes, often completed in about 3 hours 9 minutes. Touring cycling in Pyrénées Ariégeoises is defined by challenging mountain passes, tranquil greenways, and scenic river valleys. The network offers options for different ability levels, from demanding climbs to leisurely car-free paths. The routes in Pyrénées Ariégeoises are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.5 stars from over 400 reviews. More than 6000 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore Pyrénées Ariégeoises's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many no traffic touring cycling routes are available in Pyrénées Ariégeoises?
A: The Pyrénées Ariégeoises region offers a wide selection of touring cycling routes designed to minimize traffic, with over 140 routes available. These range from easy greenways to more challenging mountain passes, ensuring options for all skill levels.

Q: What is the best time of year for no traffic cycling in Pyrénées Ariégeoises?
A: The best seasons for road cycling in Pyrénées Ariégeoises are &lt;strong&gt;spring and autumn&lt;/strong&gt;. During these times, you'll experience mild weather and vibrant natural colors, making for a pleasant and scenic ride. Summer can also be enjoyable, especially at higher altitudes, but be mindful of potential heat in the valleys.

Q: Are there any easy, family-friendly no traffic cycling routes?
A: Yes, the region is known for its 'Voie Verte' (greenway) bike paths, which are ideal for families and beginners. These routes are often created on former railway lines, offering gentle slopes and picturesque views. An excellent example is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e927536779/saint-girons-foix-greenway" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Saint-Girons - Foix Greenway&lt;/a&gt;, which provides a pleasant, car-free experience.

Q: What kind of natural attractions can I see along these routes?
A: The Pyrénées Ariégeoises boast diverse natural beauty. You can expect panoramic views of mountains, deep valleys, lush forests, and winding rivers. The region also features natural monuments, waterfalls, and canyons. For example, you might encounter stunning gorges like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6134573" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cirque de Cagateille&lt;/a&gt; or explore the unique &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2204863" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Grotte du Mas-d'Azil&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there any historical sites or cultural landmarks accessible from the no traffic routes?
A: Absolutely. The region is rich in history. You can explore ancient Cathar castles, such as those mentioned in the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/guide/3856228/castles-in-pyrenees-ariegeoises" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castles in Pyrénées Ariégeoises guide&lt;/a&gt;, or charming medieval villages like Mirepoix. While cycling, you might pass by sites like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5962186" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Miglos Castle&lt;/a&gt; or the ruins of &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5174893" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Château de la Garde&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there any circular no traffic cycling routes in the area?
A: Yes, many routes are designed as loops, allowing you to start and end at the same point. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/6982336" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Vernajoul Viaduct Greenway – The tunnels of Véloroute 81 loop from Castelnau-Durban&lt;/a&gt; offers a substantial circular ride, perfect for exploring without retracing your steps.

Q: What is the average rating for touring cycling routes in Pyrénées Ariégeoises?
A: The touring cycling routes in Pyrénées Ariégeoises are highly regarded by the komoot community, with an average rating of &lt;strong&gt;4.5 out of 5 stars&lt;/strong&gt;. Reviewers often praise the tranquil experience, the stunning natural scenery, and the well-maintained paths that make for an enjoyable ride.

Q: Can I find challenging no traffic touring routes in the Pyrénées Ariégeoises?
A: While this guide focuses on 'no traffic' routes, the Pyrénées Ariégeoises are renowned for challenging mountain passes that, outside of peak season or specific events, can offer relatively quiet roads. Routes like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e931651170/discovering-notre-dame-de-la-goutte-in-montardit" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Discovering Notre-Dame de la Goutte in Montardit&lt;/a&gt; or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38688521" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Catchaudégué Pass – Col de Catchaudégué (893 m) loop from Alos&lt;/a&gt; provide significant elevation gain and a rewarding challenge for experienced cyclists.

Q: Are there places to stop for refreshments or food along the car-free bike routes?
A: Many of the greenways and routes pass through or near charming villages where you can find local cafes, bakeries, and restaurants. While specific amenities vary by route, the region's less populated nature means you'll often find authentic, welcoming establishments in towns like Foix, Saint-Girons, or Mirepoix.

Q: How accessible are these routes by public transport?
A: Public transport options to access car-free cycling routes can be limited in some of the more remote areas of Pyrénées Ariégeoises. However, larger towns like Foix and Saint-Girons, which serve as starting points for some greenways, are generally better connected by regional buses or trains. It's advisable to check local transport schedules in advance for specific route access.

Q: What are the typical lengths and durations of these no traffic touring routes?
A: The routes vary significantly in length and duration. Easy greenways might be around 20-30 km, taking a couple of hours, while more challenging loops can extend to 80 km or more, requiring a full day of cycling. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e927536779/saint-girons-foix-greenway" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Saint-Girons - Foix Greenway&lt;/a&gt; is about 29.5 km, while the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40112263" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Vernajoul Viaduct Greenway – The tunnels of Véloroute 81 loop from Saint-Jean-de-Verges&lt;/a&gt; is over 84 km.
tips: In front of us lies the main ridge of the Pyrenees on the Spanish border. The snow-capped mountains now accompany us as we cycle parallel to the Pyrenees towards the Atlantic.

The V81 is particularly pleasant, and particularly between Saint-Girons and Foix where it is often shaded, its tunnels are lit as hikers pass by. And although it can sometimes be very close to the road, the neighboring road traffic is easily overlooked.
The interest of these greenways is also to present a low inclination.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on the Saint-Girons - Foix Greenway?
faqs.answer: The greenway primarily features a hard, compacted gravel surface, with some sections that are asphalted or have gravel double tracks. While generally smooth in parts, you might encounter some rougher sections. Its origin as a former railway line means it's largely flat with very gentle gradients, making it accessible for most cyclists.

faqs.question: How long does it take to cycle the Saint-Girons - Foix Greenway?
faqs.answer: The greenway spans approximately 42 to 45.55 kilometers (27 miles). While the exact duration depends on your pace and stops, it's designed for a leisurely touring cycling experience. You can expect it to take a few hours to complete one way, allowing plenty of time to enjoy the scenery and points of interest.

faqs.question: Is the trail suitable for beginners or families?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Saint-Girons - Foix Greenway is considered an easy and comfortable route, perfect for beginners and families. Its gentle terrain and minimal elevation gain mean it doesn't present challenging climbs. It's an ideal choice for a relaxed ride with children or those new to touring cycling.

faqs.question: What kind of bike is best for this greenway?
faqs.answer: Due to the compacted dirt and gravel surface, hybrid bikes, mountain bikes, or gravel bikes are highly recommended for comfort and stability. While road cyclists can manage, wider tires would be preferable to handle any rougher sections.

faqs.question: What can I see along the route?
faqs.answer: The greenway offers diverse scenery, including wooded areas, lush meadows, and distant views of the Pyrenees. You'll pass through tranquil villages and encounter impressive structures from its railway past, such as tunnels (some with motion-activated lights) and the Vernajoul Viaduct. Don't miss the View of the Pyrenees and the greenway itself is part of the broader Piémont Pyrénéen Greenway (V81). You can also make detours to visit historical sites like Combelongue Abbey or the medieval town of Foix with its castle.

faqs.question: Where can I park to access the greenway?
faqs.answer: Parking is available at both ends of the greenway in Saint-Girons and Foix, as well as in some of the villages along the route. Specific parking areas are typically well-marked near the trailheads. For detailed information on parking options, it's advisable to check local tourism websites for Saint-Girons or Foix.

faqs.question: Is the Saint-Girons - Foix Greenway dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: Yes, dogs are generally allowed on the greenway. However, it's always best practice to keep your dog on a leash, especially in more populated areas or when passing through villages, to ensure the safety and comfort of all users.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The greenway is enjoyable for much of the year. Spring and autumn offer pleasant temperatures and beautiful scenery. Summer can be warm, but the wooded sections provide welcome shade. Winter cycling is possible, but check local weather conditions as some services might be reduced.

faqs.question: Do I need a permit or is there an entrance fee for the greenway or the Parc naturel régional des Pyrénées ariégeoises?
faqs.answer: No, there are no permits required or entrance fees to cycle the Saint-Girons - Foix Greenway. Similarly, there are no general entrance fees for the Parc naturel régional des Pyrénées ariégeoises itself, as it's a regional natural park designed for public access and enjoyment. However, specific attractions or sites along the route might have their own entry fees.

faqs.question: Does the trail intersect other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route also passes through sections of the Véloroute du Piémont Pyrénéen 06 (Saint-Girons - Foix), the Voie Verte Foix / Saint-Girons, and the Chemin du Piémont Pyrénéen. This greenway is also part of the broader Vélosud cycle route (V81).

faqs.question: Are there any tunnels on the greenway, and are they lit?
faqs.answer: Yes, there are several tunnels along the route, a notable feature from its railway past. Some of these, including a particularly long one near Rimont, are equipped with motion-activated lights for safety and convenience.
